layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Aside from their theoretical value, better meas-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ures for predicting and explaining system use

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: would have great practical value, both for ven-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: dors who would like to assess user demand for

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: new design ideas, and for information systems

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: managers within user organizations who would

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: like to evaluate these vendor offerings.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Unvalidated measures are routinely used in prac-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tice today throughout the entire spectrum of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: design, selection, implementation and evaluation

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: activities. For example: designers within vendor

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: organizations such as IBM (Gould, et al., 1983),

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Xerox (Brewley, et al., 1983), and Digital Equip-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Abstract

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Valid measurement scales for predicting user

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: acceptance of computers are in short supply.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Most subjective measures used in practice are

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: unvalidated, and their relationship to system

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: usage is unknown. The present research de-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: velops and validates new scales for two spe-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cific variables, perceived usefulness and per-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ceived ease of use, which are hypothesized to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: be fundamental determinants of user accep-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tance. Definitions for these two variables were

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: used to develop scale items that were pretested

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: for content validity and then tested for reliability

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and construct validity in two studies involving

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a total of 152 users and four application pro-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: grams. The measures were refined and stream-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: lined, resulting in two six-item scales with reli-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: abilities of .98 for usefulness and .94 for ease

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of use. The scales exhibited high convergent,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: discriminant, and factorial validity. Perceived use-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: fulness was significantly correlated with both self-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: reported current usage (r=.63, Study 1) and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: self-predicted future usage (r = .85, Study 2). Per-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ceived ease of use was also significantly corre-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: lated with current usage (r=.45, Study 1) and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: future usage (r=.59, Study 2). In both studies,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: usefulness had a significantly greater correla-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tion with usage behavior than did ease of use.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Regression analyses suggest that perceived

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ease of use may actually be a causal antece-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Information technology offers the potential for sub-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: stantially improving white collar performance

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (Curley, 1984; Edelman, 1981; Sharda, et al.,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1988). But performance gains are often ob-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: structed by users' unwillingness to accept and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: use available systems (Bowen, 1986; Young,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1984). Because of the persistence and impor-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tance of this problem, explaining user accep-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tance has been a long-standing issue in MiS

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: research (Swanson, 1974; Lucas, 1975; Schultz

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and Slevin, 1975; Robey, 1979; Ginzberg, 1981;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Swanson, 1987). Although numerous individual,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: organizational, and technological variables have

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: been investigated (Benbasat and Dexter, 1986;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Franz and Robey, 1986; Markus and Bjorn-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Anderson, 1987; Robey and Farrow, 1982), re-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: search has been constrained by the shortage

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of high-quality measures for key determinants

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of user acceptance. Past research indicates that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: many measures do not correlate highly with

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: system use (DeSanctis, 1983; Ginzberg, 1981;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Schewe, 1976; Srinivasan, 1985), and the size

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of the usage correlation varies greatly from one

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: study to the next depending on the particular

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: measures used (Baroudi, et al., 1986; Barki and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Huff, 1985; Robey, 1979; Swanson, 1982, 1987).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The development of improved measures for key

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: theoretical constructs is a research priority for

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the information systems field.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: dent to perceived usefulness, as opposed to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a parallel, direct determinant of system usage.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Implications are drawn for future research on

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: user acceptance.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Theoretical Foundations

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: IT Usefulness and Ease of Use

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: What causes people to accept or reject informa-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tion technology? Among the many variables that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: may influence system use, previous research sug-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: gests two determinants that are especially im-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: portant. First, people tend to use or not use an

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: application to the extent they believe it will help

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: them perform their job better. We refer to this

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: first variable as perceived usefulness. Second,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: even if potential users believe that a given ap-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: plication is useful, they may, at the same time,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Perceived ease of use, in contrast, refers to "the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: degree to which a person believes that using

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a particular system would be free of effort." This

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: follows from the definition of "ease": "freedom

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: from difficulty or great effort." Effort is a finite

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: resource that a person may allocate to the vari-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ous activities for which he or she is responsible

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (Radner and Rothschild, 1975). All else being

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: equal, we claim, an application perceived to be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: easier to use than another is more likely to be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: accepted by users.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: believe that the systems is too hard to use and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: that the performance benefits of usage are out-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: weighed by the effort of using the application.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: That is, in addition to usefulness, usage is theo-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: rized to be influenced by perceived ease of use.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Perceived Usefulness and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Perceived Ease of Use

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: MIS Quarterly/September 1989

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ment Corporation (Good, et al., 1986) measure

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: user perceptions to guide the development of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: new information technologies and products; in-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: dustry publications often report user surveys

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (e.g., Greenberg, 1984; Rushinek and Rushinek,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1986); several methodologies for software se-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: lection call for subjective user inputs (e.g.,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Goslar, 1986; Klein and Beck, 1987); and con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: temporary design principles emphasize meas-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: uring user reactions throughout the entire design

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: process (Anderson and Olson 1985; Gould and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Lewis, 1985; Johansen and Baker, 1984; Mantei

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and Teorey, 1988; Norman, 1983; Shneiderman,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1987). Despite the widespread use of subjec-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tive measures in practice, little attention is paid

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to the quality of the measures used or how well

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: they correlate with usage behavior. Given the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: low usage correlations often observed in re-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: search studies, those who base important busi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ness decisions on unvalidated measures may

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: be getting misinformed about a system's accept-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ability to users.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The theoretical importance of perceived useful-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ness and perceived ease of use as determinants

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of user behavior is indicated by several diverse

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: lines of research. The impact of perceived use-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: fulness on system utilization was suggested by

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the work of Schultz and Slevin (1975) and Robey

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (1979). Schultz and Slevin (1975) conducted an

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: exploratory factor analysis of 67 questionnaire

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: items, which yielded seven dimensions. Of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: these, the "performance" dimension, interpreted

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: by the authors as the perceived "effect of the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: model on the manager's job performance," was

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: most highly correlated with self-predicted use of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a decision model (r = .61). Using the Schultz and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Slevin questionnaire, Robey (1979) finds the per-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: formance dimension to be most correlated with

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: two objective measures of system usage (r = .79

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and .76). Building on Vertinsky, et al.'s (1975)

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: expectancy model, Robey (1979) theorizes that:

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: "A system that does not help people perform

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: their jobs is not likely to be received favorably

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The purpose of this research is to pursue better

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: measures for predicting and explaining use. The

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: investigation focuses on two theoretical con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: structs, perceived usefulness and perceived

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ease of use, which are theorized to be funda-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: mental determinants of system use. Definitions

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: for these constructs are formulated and the theo-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: retical rationale for their hypothesized influence

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: on system use is reviewed. New, multi-item meas-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: urement scales for perceived usefulness and per-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ceived ease of use are developed, pretested,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and then validated in two separate empirical stud-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ies. Correlation and regression analyses exam-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ine the empirical relationship between the new

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: measures and self-reported indicants of system

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: use. The discussion concludes by drawing im-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: plications for future research.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Perceived usefulness is defined here as "the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: degree to which a person believes that using

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a particular system would enhance his or her

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: job performance." This follows from the defini-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tion of the word useful: "capable of being used

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: advantageously." Within an organizational con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: text, people are generally reinforced for good

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: performance by raises, promotions, bonuses,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and other rewards (Pfeffer, 1982; Schein, 1980;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Vroom, 1964). A system high in perceived use-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: fulness, in turn, is one for which a user believes

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: in the existence of a positive use-performance

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: relationship.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: IT Usefulness and Ease of Use

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Self-efficacy theory

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to learn a computer language. The self efficacy

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: paradigm does not offer a general measure ap-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: plicable to our purposes since efficacy beliefs

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: are theorized to be situationally-specific, with

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: measures tailored to the domain under study

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (Bandura, 1982). Self efficacy research does,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: however, provide one of several theoretical per-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: pectives suggesting that perceived ease of use

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and perceived usefulness function as basic de-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: terminants of user behavior.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Hill, et al. (1987) find that both self-efficacy and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: outcome beliefs exert an influence on decisions

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Cost-benefit paradigm

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: MIS Quarterly/September 1989

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Cost-benefit research has primarily used objec-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tive measures of accuracy and effort in research

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: studies, downplaying the distinction between ob-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: jective and subjective accuracy and effort. In-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: creased emphasis on subjective constructs is war-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ranted, however, since (1) a decision maker's

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: choice of strategy is theorized to be based on

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: subjective as opposed to objective accuracy and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: effort (Beach and Mitchell, 1978), and (2) other

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: research suggests that subjective measures are

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: often in disagreement with their ojbective coun-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: terparts (Abelson and Levi, 1985; Adelbratt and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Montgomery, 1980; Wright, 1975). Introducing

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: measures of the decision maker's own perceived

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: costs and benefits, independent of the decision

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: actually made, has been suggested as a way

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of mitigating criticisms that the cost/ benefit frame-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: work is tautological (Abelson and Levi, 1985).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The distinction made herein between perceived

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: usefulness and perceived ease of use is similar

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to the distinction between subjective decision-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: making performance and effort.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: in spite of careful implementation efforts" (p.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 537). Although the perceived use-performance

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: contingency, as presented in Robey's (1979)

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: model, parallels our definition of perceived use-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: fulness, the use of Schultz and Slevin's (1975)

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: performance factor to operationalize perform-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ance expectancies is problematic for several rea-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sons: the instrument is empirically derived via

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: exploratory factor analysis; a somewhat low ratio

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of sample size to items is used (2:1); four of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: thirteen items have loadings below .5, and sev-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: eral of the items clearly fall outside the defini-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tion of expected performance improvements

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (e.g., "My job will be more satisfying," "Others

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: will be more aware of what I am doing," etc.).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: An alternative expectancy-theoretic model, de-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: rived from Vroom (1964), was introduced and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tested by DeSanctis (1983). The use-perform-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ance expectancy was not analyzed separately

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: from performance-reward instrumentalities and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: reward valences. Instead, a matrix-oriented meas-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: urement procedure was used to produce an over-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: all index of "motivational force" that combined

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: these three constructs. "Force" had small but

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: significant correlations with usage of a DSS

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: within a business simulation experiment (corre-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: lations ranged from .04 to .26). The contrast be-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tween DeSanctis's correlations and the ones ob-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: served by Robey underscore the importance of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: measurement in predicting and explaining use.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The importance of perceived ease of use is sup-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ported by Bandura's (1982) extensive research

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: on self-efficacy, defined as "judgments of how

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: well one can execute courses of action required

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to deal with prospective situations" (p. 122). Self-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: efficacy is similar to perceived ease of use as

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: defined above. Self-efficacy beliefs are theorized

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to function as proximal determinants of behav-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ior. Bandura's theory distinguishes self-efficacy

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: judgments from outcome judgments, the latter

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: being concerned with the extent to which a be-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: havior, once successfully executed, is believed

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to be linked to valued outcomes. Bandura's "out-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: come judgment" variable is similar to perceived

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: usefulness. Bandura argues that self-efficacy

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and outcome beliefs have differing antecedents

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and that, "In any given instance, behavior would

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: be best predicted by considering both self-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: efficacy and outcome beliefs" (p. 140).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The cost-benefit paradigm from behavioral deci-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sion theory (Beach and Mitchell, 1978; Johnson

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and Payne, 1985; Payne, 1982) is also relevant

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to perceived usefulness and ease of use. This

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: research explains people's choice among vari-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ous decision-making strategies (such as linear

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: compensatory, conjunctive, disjunctive and elmi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: nation-by-aspects) in terms of a cognitive trade-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: off between the effort required to employ the strat-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: egy and the quality (accuracy) of the resulting

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: decision. This approach has been effective for

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: explaining why decision makers alter their choice

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: strategies in response to changes in task com-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: plexity. Although the cost-benefit approach has

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: mainly concerned itself with unaided decision

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: making, recent work has begun to apply the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: same form of analysis to the effectiveness of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: information display formats (Jarvenpaa, 1989;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Kleinmuntz and Schkade, 1988).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Channel disposition model

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Non-MIS studies

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: IT Usefulness and Ease of Use

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Swanson (1982, 1987) introduced and tested a

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: model of "channel disposition" for explaining the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: choice and use of information reports. The con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cept of channel disposition is defined as having

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Evaluation of information reports

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Adoption of innovations

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: MIS Quarterly/September 1989

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Research on the adoption of innovations also

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: suggests a prominent role for perceived ease

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of use. In their meta-analysis of the relationship

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: between the characteristics of an innovation and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: its adoption, Tornatzky and Klein (1982) find that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: compatibility, relative advantage, and complex-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ity have the most consistent significant relation-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ships across a broad range of innovation types.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Complexity, defined by Rogers and Shoemaker

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (1971) as "the degree to which an innovation

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: is perceived as relatively difficult to understand

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and use" (p. 154), parallels perceived ease of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: use quite closely. As Tornatzky and Klein (1982)

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: point out, however, compatibility and relative ad-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: vantage have both been dealt with so broadly

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and inconsistently in the literature as to be diffi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cult to interpret.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Outside the MIS domain, a marketing study by

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Hauser and Simmie (1981) concerning user per-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ceptions of alternative communication technolo-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: gies similarly derived two underlying dimensions:

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ease of use and effectiveness, the latter being

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: similar to the perceived usefulness construct de-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: fined above. Both ease of use and effectiveness

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: were influential in the formation of user prefer-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ences regarding a set of alternative communi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cation technologies. The human-computer inter-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: action (HCl) research community has heavily

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: emphasized ease of use in design (Branscomb

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and Thomas, 1984; Card, et al., 1983; Gould

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and Lewis, 1985). For the most part, however,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: these studies have focused on objective meas-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ures of ease of use, such as task completion

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: time and error rates. In many vendor organiza-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tions, usability testing has become a standard

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: phase in the product development cycle, with

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Past research within MIS on the evaluation of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: information reports echoes the distinction be-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tween usefulness and ease of use made herein.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Larcker and Lessig (1980) factor analyzed six

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: items used to rate four information reports. Three

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: items load on each of two distinct factors: (1)

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: perceived importance, which Larcker and Lessig

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: define as "the quality that causes a particular

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: information set to acquire relevance to a deci-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sion maker," and the extent to which the infor-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: mation elements are "a necessary input for task

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: accomplishment," and (2) perceived usable-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ness, which is defined as the degree to which

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: "the information format is unambiguous, clear

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: or readable" (p. 123). These two dimensions are

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: similar to perceived usefulness and perceived

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ease of use as defined above, repsectively, al-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: though Larcker and Lessig refer to the two di-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: mensions collectively as "perceived usefulness."

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Reliabilities for the two dimensions fall in the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: range of .64-.77, short of the .80 minimal level

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: recommended for basic research. Correlations

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: with actual use of information reports were not

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: addressed in their study.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: two components: attributed information quality

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and attributed access quality. Potential users are

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: hypothesized to select and use information re-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ports based on an implicit psychological trade-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: off between information quality and associated

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: costs of access. Swanson (1987) performed an

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: exploratory factor analysis in order to measure

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: information quality and access quality. A five-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: factor solution was obtained, with one factor cor-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: responding to information quality (Factor #3,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: "value"), and one to access quality (Factor #2,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: accessibility"). Inspecting the items that load on

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: these factors suggests a close correspondence

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to perceived usefulness and ease of use. Items

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: such as "important," "relevant," "useful," and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: "valuable" load strongly on the value dimension.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Thus, value parallels perceived usefulness. The

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: fact that relevance and usefulness load on the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: same factor agrees with information scientists,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: who emphasize the conceptual similarity be-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tween the usefulness and relevance notions

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (Saracevic, 1975). Several of Swanson's "acces-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sibility" items, such as "convenient," "controlla-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ble," "easy," and "unburdensome," correspond

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to perceived ease of use as defined above. Al-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: though the study was more exploratory than con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: firmatory, with no attempts at construct valida-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tion, it does agree with the conceptual distinction

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: between usefulness and ease of use. Self-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: reported information channel use correlated .20

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: with the value dimension and .13 with the ac-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cessibility dimension.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Scale Development and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Pretest

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: IT Usefulness and Ease of Use

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: large investments in test facilities and instrumen-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tation. Although objective ease of use is clearly

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: relevant to user performance given the system

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: is used, subjective ease of use is more relevant

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to the users' decision whether or not to use the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: system and may not agree with the objective

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: measures (Carroll and Thomas, 1988).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The initial item pools for perceived usefulness

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and perceived ease of use are given in Tables

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Convergence of findings

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: MIS Quarterly/September 1989

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Psychometricians emphasize that the validity of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a measurement scale is built in from the outset.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: As Nunnally (1978) points out, "Rather than test

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the validity of measures after they have been

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: constructed, one should ensure the validity by

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the plan and procedures for construction" (p.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 258). Careful selection of the initial scale items

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: helps to assure the scales will possess "content

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: validity," defined as "the degree to which the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: score or scale being used represents the con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cept about which generalizations are to be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: made" (Bohrnstedt, 1970, p. 91). In discussing

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: content validity, psychometricians often appeal

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to the "domain sampling model," (Bohrnstedt,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1970; Nunnally, 1978) which assumes there is

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a domain of content corresponding to each vari-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: able one is interested in measuring. Candidate

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: items representative of the domain of content

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: should be selected. Researchers are advised to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: begin by formulating conceptual definitions of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: what is to be measured and preparing items to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: fit the construct definitions (Anastasi, 1986).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Following these recommendations, candidate

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: items for perceived usefulness and perceived

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ease of use were generated based on their con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ceptual definitions, stated above, and then pre-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tested in order to select those items that best

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: fit the content domains. The Spearman-Brown

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Prophecy formula was used to choose the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: number of items to generate for each scale. This

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: formula estimates the number of items needed

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to achieve a given reliability based on the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: number of items and reliability of comparable

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: existing scales. Extrapolating from past studies,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the formula suggests that 10 items would be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: needed for each perceptual variable to achieve

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: reliability of at least .80 (Davis, 1986). Adding

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: four additional items for each construct to allow

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: for item elimination, it was decided to generate

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 14 items for each construct.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: From multiple disciplinary vantage points, per-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ceived usefulness and perceived ease of use

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: are indicated as fundamental and distinct con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: structs that are influential in decisions to use in-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: formation technology. Although certainly not the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: only variables of interest in explaining user be-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: havior (for other variables, see Cheney, et al.,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1986; Davis, et al., 1989; Swanson, 1988), they

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: do appear likely to play a central role. Improved

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: measures are needed to gain further insight into

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the nature of perceived usefulness and per-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ceived ease of use, and their roles as determi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: nants of computer use.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: retained, yielding 10 items for each construct.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Next, a field study (Study 1) of 112 users con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cerning two different interactive computer sys-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tems was conducted in order to assess the reli-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ability and construct validity of the resulting

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: scales. The scales were further refined and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: streamlined to six items per construct. A lab

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: study (Study 2) involving 40 participants and two

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: graphics systems was then conducted. Data

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: from the two studies were then used to assess

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the relationship between usefulness, ease of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: use, and self-reported usage.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: There is a striking convergence among the wide

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: range of theoretical perspectives and research

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: studies discussed above. Although Hill, et al.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (1987) examined learning a computer language,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Larcker and Lessig (1980) and Swanson (1982,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1987) dealt with evaluating information reports,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and Hauser and Simmie (1981) studied com-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: munication technologies, all are supportive of the

layout_dets.text: fulness and ease of use. The accumulated body

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: fulness and ease of use as key determinants

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of behavior.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.text: that best fit the definitions of the constructs were

layout_dets.text: 1 and 2, respectively. In preparing candidate

layout_dets.text: viewed in other to identify various facets of the

layout_dets.text: constructs that should be measured (Davis,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: electronic mail system," which is one of the two

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: test applications investigated in Study 1, reported

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: below. The items within each pool tend to have

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a lot of overlap in their meaning, which is con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sistent with the fact that they are intended as

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: measures of the same underlying construct.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Though different individuals may attribute slightly

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: different meaning to particular item statements,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the goal of the multi-item approach is to reduce

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: any extranneous effects of individual items, al-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: lowing idiosyncrasies to be cancelled out by

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Pretest interviews were performed to further en-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: hance content validity by assessing the corre-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: spondence between candidate items and the defi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: measure. Items that don't represent a construct's

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: individuals to rank the degree to which each item

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ing items receiving low rankings. In eliminating

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: items, we want to make sure not to reduce the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The resulting rank and cluster data are summa-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: rized in Tables 3 (usefulness) and 4 (ease of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: use). For perceived usefulness, notice that items

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: fall into three main clusters. The first cluster re-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: lates to job effectiveness, the second to produc-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tivity and time savings, and the third to the im-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: portance of the system to one's job. If we

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: eliminate the lowest-ranked items (items 1, 4,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 5 and 9), we see that the three major clusters

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: each have at least two items. Item 2, "control

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: others. By asking individuals to rate the similar-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ity of items to one another, we can perform a

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cluster analysis to determine the structure of the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: substrata, remove items where excess coverage

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: is suggested, and add items where inadequate

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: coverage is indicated.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: over work" was retained since, although it was

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ranked fairly low, it fell in the top 9 and may

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tap an important aspect of usefulness.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: MIS Quarterly/September 1989

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: For the categorization task, participants were

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: asked to put the 13 cards into three to five cate-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: gories so that the statements within a category

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: were most similar in meaning to each other and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: dissimilar in meaning from those in other cate-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: gories. This was an adaptation of the "own cate-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: gories" procedure of Sherif and Sherif (1967).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Categorization provides a simple indicant of simi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: larity that requires less time and effort to obtain

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: than other similarity measurement procedures

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: such as paid comparisons. The similarity data

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: was cluster analyzed by assigning to the same

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cluster items that seven or more subjects placed

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: in the same category. The clusters are consid-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ered to be a reflection of the domain substrata

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: for each construct and serve as a basis of as-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sessing coverage, or representativeness, of the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: item pools.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: In this study, therefore, ease of learning is re-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: garded as one substratum of the ease of use

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: construct, as opposed to a distinct construct.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Since items 4 and 13 provide a rather indirect

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: assessment of ease of learning, they were re-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: placed with two items that more directly get at

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ease of learning: "Learning to operate the elec-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tronic mail system is easy for me," and "I find

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: it takes a lot of effort to become skillful at using

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: electronic mail." Items 6, 9 and 2 were elimi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: nated because they did not cluster with other

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: items, and they received low priority rankings,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: which suggests that they do not fit well within

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the content domain for ease of use. Together

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: with the "overall" items for each construct, this

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: procedure yielded a 10-item scale for each con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: struct to be empirically tested for reliability and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: construct validity.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Pretest participants consisted of a sample of 15

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: experienced computer users from the Sloan

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: School of Management, MIT, including five sec-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: retaries, five graduate students and five mem-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: bers of the professional staff. In face-to-face in-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: terviews, participants were asked to perform two

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tasks, prioritization and categorization, which

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: were done separately for usefulness and ease

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of use. For prioritization, they were first given

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a card containing the definition of the target con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: struct and asked to read it. Next, they were given

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 13 index cards each having one of the items

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: for that construct written on it. The 14th or "over-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: all" item for each construct was omitted since

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: its wording was almost identical to the label on

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the definition card (see Tables 1 and 2). Partici-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: pants were asked to rank the 13 cards accord-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ing to how well the meaning of each statement

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: matched the given definition of ease of use or

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: usefulness.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Looking now at perceived ease of use (Table

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 4), we again find three main clusters. The first

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: relates to physical effort, while the second re-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: lates to mental effort. Selecting the six highest-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: priority items and eliminating the seventh pro-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: vides good coverage of these two clusters. Item

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 11 ("understandable") was reworded to read

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: "clear and understandable" in an effort to pick

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: up some of the content of item 1 ("confusing"),

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: which has been eliminated. The third cluster is

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: somewhat more difficult to interpret but appears

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to be tapping perceptions of how easy a system

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: is to learn. Remembering how to perform tasks,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: using the manual, and relying on system guid-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ance are all phenomena associated with the proc-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ess of learning to use a new system (Nickerson,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1981; Roberts and Moran, 1983). Further review

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of the literature suggests that ease of use and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ease of learning are strongly related. Roberts

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and Moran (1983) find a correlation of .79 be-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tween objective measures of ease of use and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ease of learning. Whiteside, et al. (1985) find

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: that ease of use and ease of learning are

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: strongly related and conclude that they are con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: gruent. Studies of how people learn new sys-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tems suggest that learning and using are not

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: separate, disjoint activities, but instead that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: people are motivated to begin performing actual

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: work directly and try to "learn by doing" as op-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: posed to going through user manuals or online

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tutorials (Carroll and Carrithers, 1984; Carroll,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: et al., 1985; Carroll and McKendree,, 1987).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: A field study was conducted to assess the reli-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ability, convergent validity, discriminant validity,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and factorial validity of the 10-item scales re-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sulting from the pretest. A sample of 120 users

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: within IBM Canada's Toronto Development Labo-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ratory were given a questionnaire asking them

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to rate the usefulness and ease of use of two

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: systems available there: PROFS electronic mail

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and the XEDIT file editor. The computing envi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ronment consisted of IBM mainframes accessi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ble through 327X terminals. The PROFS elec-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tronic mail system is a simple but limited

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: messaging facility for brief messages. (See

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Panko, 1988.) The XEDIT editor is widely avail-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: able on IBM systems and offers both full-screen

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and command-driven editing capabilities. The

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: questionnaire asked participants to rate the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: extent to which they agree with each statement

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: by circling a number from one to seven arranged

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: horizontally beneath anchor point descriptions

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Strongly Agree,""Neutral," and "Strongly Dis

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: agree." In order to ensure subject familiarity with

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the systems being rated, instructions asked the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: participants to skip over the section pertaining

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to a given system if they never use it. Responses

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: were obtained from 112 participants, for a re-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sponse rate of 93%. Of these 112, 109 were

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: users of electronic mail and 75 were users of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: XEDIT. Subjects had an average of six months'

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: experience with the two systems studied. Among

layout_dets.text: the sample, 10 percent were managers, 35 per-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cent were administrative staff, and 55 percent

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: were professional staff (which included a broad

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: mix of market analysts, product development ana-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: lysts, programmers, financial analysts and re-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: search scientists).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The perceived usefulness scale attained Cron-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: bach alpha reliability of .97 for both the elec-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tronic mail and XEDiT systems, while perceived

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ease of use achieved a reliability of .86 for elec-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tronic mail and .93 for XEDIT. When observa-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tions were pooled for the two systems, alpha

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: was .97 for usefulness and .91 for ease of use.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Factorial validity is concerned with whether the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: usefulness and ease of use items form distinct

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: constructs. A principal components analysis

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: using oblique rotation was performed on the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: twenty usefulness and ease of use items. Data

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: were pooled across the two systems, for a total

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of 184 observations. The results show that the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: MIS Quarterly/September 1989

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Convergent and discriminant validity were tested

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: using multitrait-multimethod (MTMM) analysis

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (Campbell and Fiske, 1959). The MTMM matrix

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: contains the intercorrelations of items (methods)

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: applied to the two different test systems (traits),

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: electronic mail and XEDIT. Convergent validity

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: refers to whether the items comprising a scale

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: behave as if they are measuring a common un-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: derlying construct. In order to demonstrate con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: vergent validity, items that measure the same

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: trait should correlate highly with one another

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (Campbell and Fiske, 1959). That is, the ele-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ments in the monotrait triangles (the submatrix

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of intercorrelations between items intended to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: measure the same construct for the same

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: system) within the MTMM matrices should be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: large. For perceived usefulness, the 90 monotrait-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: heteromethod correlations were all significant at

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the .05 level. For ease of use, 86 out of 90,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: or 95.6%, of the monotrait-heteromethod corre-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: lations were significant. Thus, our data supports

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the convergent validity of the two scales.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The few exceptions to the convergent and dis-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: criminant validity that did occur, although not ex-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tensive enough to invalidate the ease of use

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: scale, all involved negatively phrased ease of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: use items. These "reversed" items tended to cor-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: relate more with the same item used to meas-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ure a different trait than they did with other items

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of the same trait, suggesting the presence of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: common method variance. This is ironic, since

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: reversed scales are typically used in an effort

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to reduce common method variance. Silk (1971)

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: similarly observed minor departures from con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: vergent and discriminant validity for reversed

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: items. The five positively worded ease of use

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: items had a reliability of .92 compared to .83

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: for the five negative items. This suggests an im-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: provement in the ease of use scale may be pos-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sible with the elimination or reversal of nega-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tively phrased items. Nevertheless, the MTMM

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: analysis supported the ability of the 10-item

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: scales for each construct to differentiate between

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: systems.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: different trait or with different items used to meas-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ure a different trait (Campbell and Fiske, 1959).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: For perceived usefulness, 1,800 such compari-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sons were confirmed without exception. Of the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1,800 comparisons for ease of use there were

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 58 exceptions (3%). This represents an unusu-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ally high level of discriminant validity (Campbell

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and Fiske, 1959; Silk, 1971) and implies that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the usefulness and ease of use scales possess

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a high concentration of trait variance and are

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: not strongly influenced by methodological

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: artifacts.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Table 5 gives a summary frequency table of the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: correlations comprising the MTMM matrices for

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: usefulness and ease of use. From this table it

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: is possible to see the separation in magnitude

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: between monotrait and heterotrait correlations.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The frequency table also shows that the hetero-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: trait-heteromethod correlations do not appear to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: be substantially elevated above the heterotrait-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: monomethod correlations. This is an additional

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: diagnostic suggested by Campbell and Fiske

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (1959) to detect the presence of method

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: variance.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Discriminant validity is concerned with the abil-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ity of a measurement item to differentiate be-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tween objects being measured. For instance,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: within the MTMM matrix, a perceived usefulness

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: item applied to electronic mail should not corre-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: late too highly with the same item applied to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: XEDIT. Failure to discriminate may suggest the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: presence of "common method variance," which

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: means that an item is measuring methodological

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: artifacts unrelated to the target construct (such

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: as individual differences in the style of respond-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ing to questions (see Campbell, et al., 1967; Silk,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1971) ). The test for discriminant validity is that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: an item should correlate more highly with other

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: items intended to measure the same trait than

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: with either the same item used to measure a

layout_dets.text: In applied testing situations, it is important to

layout_dets.text: and b= .07 pooled). In other words, the signifi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Based on partial correlation analyses, the vari-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: manuals for the two products, while equalizing

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the style of writing and eliminating value state-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ments (e.g., "See how easy that was to do?").

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Half of the participants tried Chart-Master first

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and half tried Pendraw first. After using each

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: package, a questionnaire was completed.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: MIS Quarterly/September 1989

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The study involved evaluating two IBM PC-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: based graphics systems: Chart-Master (by De-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cision Resources, Inc. of Westport, CN) and Pen-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: draw (by Pencept, Inc. of Waltham, MA). Chart-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Master is a menu-driven package that creates

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: numerical business graphs, such as bar charts,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: line charts, and pie charts based on parameters

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: defined by the user. Through the keyboard and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: menus, the user inputs the data for, and defines

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the desired characteristics of, the chart to be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: made. The user can specify a wide variety of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: options relating to title fonts, colors, plot orienta-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tion, cross-hatching pattern, chart format, and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: so on. The chart can then be previewed on the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: screen, saved, and printed. Chart-Master is a

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: successful commercial product that typifies the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: category of numeric business charting programs.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The lab study involved 40 voluntary participants

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: who were evening MBA students at Boston Uni-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: versity. They were paid $25 for participating in

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the study. They had an average of five years'

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: work experience and were employed full-time in

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: several industries, including education (10 per-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cent), government (10 percent), financial (28 per-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cent), health (18 percent), and manufacturing (8

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: percent). They had a range of prior experience

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: with computers in general (35 percent none or

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: limited; 48 percent moderate; and 17 percent

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: extensive) and personal computers in particular

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (35 percent none or limited; 48 percent moder-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ate; and 15 percent extensive) but were unfa-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: miliar with the two systems used in the study.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: A lab study was performed to evaluate the six-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: item usefulness and ease of use scales result-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ing from scale refinement in Study 1. Study 2

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: was designed to approximate applied prototype

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: testing or system selection situations, an impor-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tant class of situations where measures of this

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: kind are likely to be used in practice. In proto-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: type testing and system selection contexts, pro-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: spective users are typically given a brief hands-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: on demonstration involving less than an hour of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: actually interacting with the candidate system.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Thus, representative users are asked to rate the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: future usefulness and ease of use they would

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: expect based on relatively little experience with

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the systems being rated. We are especially in-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: terested in the properties of the usefulness and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ease of use scales when they are worded in

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a prospective sense and are based on limited

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: experience with the target systems. Favorable

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: psychometric properties under these circum-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: stances would be encouraging relative to their

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: use as early warning indicants of user accep-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tance (Ginzberg, 1981).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Pendraw is quite different from the typical busi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ness charting program. It uses bit-mapped graph-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ics and a "direct manipulation" interface where

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: users draw desired shapes using a digitizer

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tablet and an electronic "pen" as a stylus. The

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: digitizer tablet supplants the keyboard as the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: input medium. By drawing on a tablet, the user

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: manipulates the image, which is visible on the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: screen as it is being created. Pendraw offers

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: capabilities typical of PC-based, bit-mapped

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: "paint" programs (see Panko, 1988), allowing

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the user to perform freehand drawing and select

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: from among geometric shapes, such as boxes,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: lines, and circles. A variety of line widths, color

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: selections and title fonts are available. The

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: digitizer is also capable of performing character

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: recognition, converting hand-printer characters

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: into various fonts (Ward and Blesser, 1985).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Pencept had positioned the Pendraw product to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: complete with business charting programs. The

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: manual introduces Pendraw by guiding the user

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: through the process of creating a numeric bar

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: chart. Thus, a key marketing issue was the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: extent to which the new product would compete

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: favorably with established brands, such as Chart-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Master.
